[[File:Korail Class 311000 2019 July 27.webm|thumb|Seoul Subway Line 1 from above, 2019]] '''Seoul Subway Line 1''' (dubbed ''The Dark Blue Line'') of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] is a [[rapid transit]] and [[commuter rail]] line which links central [[Seoul]], [[South Korea]] to [[Yeoncheon station|Yeoncheon]] in the northeast, [[Incheon]] in the southwest, and [[Sinchang station|Sinchang]] via [[Suwon]] and [[Cheonan]] in the south. The central underground portion of Line 1, running underneath [[Sejongno]], [[Jongno]], and [[Wangsan-ro]] avenues along Seoul's traditional downtown area, is the oldest subway-operated section in the Seoul Metropolitan Subway system. Its branches and services cover a large part of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Area]]; totaling {{cvt|218.3|km|mi}} in route length. The underground section between [[Seoul station]] and [[Cheongnyangni station]], which is referred to as '''Seoul Metro Line 1''' ({{convert|7.8|km|mi|sp=us|abbr=on|disp=sqbr}}) after its operator, is currently operated by [[Seoul Metro]], and is [[Railway electrification|electrified]] at the subway standard of 1.5 kV DC.{{cite web |url=http://infra.seoul.go.kr/archives/1092 |script-title=ko:지하철건설현황 |publisher=Seoul City Government |language=ko |trans-title=Status of Subway Construction |access-date=22 February 2014 |archive-date=3 January 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160103184202/http://infra.seoul.go.kr/archives/1092 |url-status=dead }} The line first opened in 1974 as the '''Korean National Railroad of Seoul''' with [[Through train|through services]] to newly-electrified (at the mainline [[Korea National Railway|KNR]] standard of 25 kV AC 60 Hz) national mainline railways from Seongbuk station (now: [[Kwangwoon University station]]) to [[Incheon station|Incheon]] and [[Suwon station]]s.{{cite web|url=https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/news/nation/2011/02/117_81284.html|title=Seoul's first subway line opened in 1974|publisher=Korea Times|access-date=25 July 2014|date=13 February 2011}} This was the first electrified rapid transit line and service in South Korea and the second in the Korean Peninsula after the [[Pyongyang Metro]] in [[North Korea]] opened the year before. Frequent service is provided between Soyosan, Dongducheon, Uijeongbu, Cheongnyangni, Seoul, Yongsan, and Guro, where trains split between Incheon in the west and Byeongjeom and Cheonan in the south. Express trains operate from Yongsan and Seoul stations to [[Dongincheon station|Dongincheon]] and [[Sinchang station|Sinchang]] stations. Trains run every 3-6 min in the central route between Seoul Station and Cheongnyangni, every 6-9 min between Seoul and Guro, every 8-10 min between Guro and Incheon and between Guro and Byeongjeom, every 10-15 min from Byeongjeom to Cheonan, and every 35 min between Cheonan and Sinchang and service up to the Dongducheon area and Soyosan station. Service to Yeoncheon is approximately once per hour, limited due to the single track section approaching Soyosan station. This service is meant to be the replacement for the Gyeongwon line commuter trains, which began at Dongducheon station prior to its closure. Trains travel along [[Gyeongbu Line|Gyeongbu]] (Seoul-Cheonan), [[Gyeongin Line|Gyeongin]] (Guro-Incheon), [[Janghang Line|Janghang]] (Cheonan-Sinchang), and [[Gyeongwon Line|Gyeongwon]] (Hoegi-Yeoncheon) railway lines. The line runs on the [[Left hand drive|left-hand]] side of the track, as opposed to the [[Right hand drive|right-hand]] side of the track like all other Seoul Metropolitan Subway lines (another exception to this is [[Seoul Subway Line 4]] although it runs on the [[Right hand drive|right-hand]] side until [[Namtaeryeong station]], and operates on the [[Left hand drive|left-hand]] side for the parts afterwards){{in lang|ko}} [http://news.naver.com/main/read.nhn?mode=LSD&mid=sec&sid1=110&oid=021&aid=0000149835 <오후여담>좌측통행], Munhwailbo since railways in Korea generally run on the left side. == Rapid (Express) trains == Korail operates a variety of express "rapid" ([[Korean language|Korean]]: 급행 ''geup-haeng'', [[Hanja]]: 急行) trains for long distance commuter services on Line 1. These services include: * Gyeongin line express services from Yongsan to Dongincheon, operating express between Guro and Dongincheon, introduced on January 29, 1999, following quadruple-tracking of the Gyeongin Line. * Gyeongbu line express services from Cheongnyangni to Cheonan or Sinchang, operating express between Gasan Digital Complex and Cheonan and running local elsewhere. * Green Gyeongbu line express service from Seoul Station to Cheonan or Sinchang, originating at a platform adjacent to Seoul Station, skipping all stations between [[Seoul station]] and [[Geumcheon-gu Office station]], making intermediate stops at Anyang, Gunpo, Uiwang, and Sungkyunkwan University, and then following the red Gyeongbu line express service pattern south of Suwon. Northbound services (departing from Cheonan/Sinchang and terminating at Seoul Station) stop at [[Yeongdeungpo station]], but southbound services are unable to due to the lack of a raised platform. This service was introduced on September 25, 1982.{{Cite web|url=https://newslibrary.naver.com/viewer/index.nhn?articleId=1982101400209210002&editNo=2&printCount=1&publishDate=1982-10-14&officeId=00020&pageNo=10&printNo=18778&publishType=00020M|title = 20일부터 하루3회 서울驛(역)~水原(수원) 직통電鐵(전철)운행}} * Gyeongwon line express services from Dongducheon to Incheon, operating express between Dongducheon and Kwangwoon University and running local between Incheon and Kwangwoon University. Former express services include: * One late night Gyeongin line express service that originated at Soyosan, made all stops to Guro, then made express stops until Incheon. * Yeongdeungpo-Byeongjeom Gyeongbu line express service, skipping all stations between [[Yeongdeungpo station|Yeongdeungpo]] and [[Anyang station|Anyang]] and then following the Yeongsan-Cheonan/Sinchang express service pattern south of Anyang until [[Byeongjeom station|Byeongjeom]]. This service was introduced on August 25, 2014 and was discontinued on December 9, 2016. * Two faster Yongsan-Sinchang limited express trains (one in each direction), introduced in 2013. This train only operated on weekends and holidays. It was discontinued on July 1, 2018. * Faster Yongsan-Dongincheon [[Limited express#South Korea|limited express]] ([[Korean language|Korean]]: 특급 ''teuk-geup'', [[Hanja]]: 特急) trains, introduced on July 7, 2017. These were discontinued on May 26, 2026.https://www.newspim.com/news/view/20260520000969 == Electrification == Seoul Subway Line 1 is powered by 1500 V direct current on the underground tunnel segment between [[Cheongnyangni station]] and [[Seoul station]], and by 25000 V alternating current (60 Hertz frequency) on the rest of the line. Underground DC system was implemented in order to prevent possible telecommunication malfunction by AC current on telephone lines, following the opposition from the Ministry of Postal Service (currently [[Ministry of Science and ICT]]).{{cite web |title=서울의 지하철(Subway of Seoul) |url=https://museum.seoul.go.kr/www/board/NR_boardView.do?colExt8_1=&q_bookCate=&colExt8_3=&actionMethod=&sortName=&searchVal=&colExt8_4=&actionUri=&bbsCd=1012&endDt=&searchKey=TITLE___1002&sso=ok®Pwd=N&colExt8=&pageType=&sortOrder=&startDt=¤tPage=&seq=20241128162557242&ctgCd=&showSummaryYn=N |website=Seoul Museum of History |access-date=13 January 2025 |pages=50 |language=Korean |format=E-book, PDF of the exhibition |date=2024-11-28}} Because of this different manner of offering electric current, there are [[Overhead line#Neutral section (phase break)|neutral sections]] between Cheongnyangni station and [[Hoegi station]], as well as between Seoul Station and [[Namyeong station]]. All Line 1 rolling stock are similarly required to be [[Multi-system (rail)|multi-system compatible]] with both electrification systems.{{cite journal |last1=Lee |first1=Hae-Won |display-authors=et al. |title=Case Study of Railway Power supply Modification(DC→AC) |journal=2019 Spring Conference for the Korean Society for Railway |date=19 April 2019 |pages=269–272 |url=https://www.dbpia.co.kr/journal/articleDetail?nodeId=NODE08757114 |language=Korean |access-date=13 January 2025}} [[File:Cheongnyangni-Hoegi DC-AC Deadsection with Seoul Metro 1009 Bound the Cheongnyangni.JPG|thumb|Cheongnyangni-Hoegi DC-AC neutral section]] == Stations == {{Unreferenced section|date=August 2023}} Until 2000, the {{convert|7.8|km|mi|abbr=on|sp=us|disp=}} underground city-center portion run by [[Seoul Metro Corporation]]—one of Seoul Metro's predecessors—was the only section properly referred to as Line 1 and labeled red on maps. On the other hand, the remaining sections of the rail line run by [[Korail]] were referred to as Korean National Railroad of Seoul and the local all-stop services labeled either blue or gray on maps, while the express services were labelled thin red. Since 2000, all adjacent through-running Korail services from the [[Gyeongbu Line|Gyeongbu]], [[Gyeongin Line|Gyeongin]] and [[Gyeongwon Line|Gyeongwon]] Lines, as well as later Korail-operated extensions and branches, are officially bundled together with the aforementioned underground portion as part of a greater Line 1, and the labeling on maps was changed and standardized to the current dark blue color (as illustrated in the map below); also to avoid confusion with [[Seoul Subway Line 4|Line 4]]'s light blue color. (A slightly different shade of red is now used to label the [[Shinbundang Line]] which opened in 2011.) {{Panorama | image = File:Seoul Subway Line 1 (ENG).svg | height = 550 | width = | alt = }} === Main Lines === Local lines: * Yeoncheon, Soyosan, Dongducheon, Yangju, Uijeongbu, Dongmyo or Guro — Incheon * Uijeongbu (3 times only on weekdays), Kwangwoon Univ., Cheongnyangni or Guro — Cheonan, Sinchang, or Seodongtan * Yeongdeungpo — Gwangmyeong (shuttle) Express lines: * '''''GW''': Gyeongwon Express (Dongducheon - Incheon)'' * '''''GI''': Gyeongin Express (Yongsan – Dongincheon)'' * '''''GB A''': Gyeongbu Express (Cheongnyangni – Cheonan or Sinchang)'' * '''''GB B''': Gyeongbu Express (Seoul – Cheonan or Sinchang)'' ※ Only [[Korail]] trains are used for express trains.

[[Korail]] is named '''Korean National Railroad''', with Line 1 just referring to the Seoul-Cheongnyangni section. 1978 :December 9: The Gyeongwon Line from [[Yongsan station|Yongsan]] to Seongbuk opens as a new branch, with [[Ichon station|Ichon]] and [[Eungbong station|Seongsu]] (now part of the Jungang Line). 1979 :February 1: [[Sungkyunkwan University station|Yuljeon station]] is opened. 1980 :January 5: [[Sinimun station]] is opened. :April 1: [[Seobinggo station|Seobinggo]], [[Hannam station|Hannam]] and [[Hoegi station|Hoegi]] (now part of the Jungang Line) are opened. :July 10: Seongsu is renamed ''[[Eungbong station]]'' 1982 :August 2: [[Seoksu station]] is opened. :September 25: Gyeongbu Express was launched between Seoul and Suwon. 1984 :January 1: [[Sungkyunkwan University station|Yuljeon station]] is renamed '''Seongdae-ap station'''. :May 22: [[Sindorim station]] is opened. :November 20: [[Baegun station]] is opened. 1985 :January 14: [[Seokgye station]] is opened. :April 20: [[Chang-dong station|Chang-dong]] is opened as a northward extension. :August 22: [[Wolgye station|Wolgye]] and [[Nokcheon station|Nokcheon]] are opened. :October 18: [[Oksu station]] (now part of the Jungang Line) is opened. 1986 :September 2: 6 stations from [[Uijeongbu station|Uijeongbu]] to [[Chang-dong station|Chang-dong]] are opened as a northward extension. 1987 :October 5: [[Ganeung station|Uijeongbu Bukbu station]] is opened. :December 31: [[Jung-dong station (Bucheon)|Jung-dong station]] is opened. 1988 :January 16: [[Onsu station]] is opened. :October 25: The Ansan Line is opened as a southward branch, from [[Geumjeong station]] to [[Ansan station]]. 1994 :July 11: [[Ganseok station|Ganseok]] and [[Dowon station|Dowon]] are opened. :December 1: [[Sungkyunkwan University station|Seongdae-ap station]] is renamed to '''Sungkyunkwan University'''. 1995 :February 16: [[Guil station]] is opened. 1996 :January 1: [[Hankuk University of Foreign Studies station|Hwigyeong station]] is renamed to '''Hankuk University of Foreign Studies station''' :March 28: [[Bugae station]] is opened. 1997 :April 30: [[Sosa station]] is opened. 1998 :January 7: [[Singil station|Singil]] and [[Doksan station|Doksan]] are opened. 2000 :'''Korea National Railroad''' and Line 1 are integrated as Seoul (Metropolitan) Subway Line 1. 2001 :November 30: [[Dohwa station]] is opened. 2003 :April 30: [[Seryu station|Seryu]] and [[Byeongjeom station|Byeongjeom]] are opened as a southward extension. The Ansan Line service is replaced with an extended [[Seoul Subway Line 4]]. 2004 :August 25: Bugok station is renamed [[Uiwang station]]. 2005 :January 20: 8 stations from [[Byeongjeom station|Byeongjeom]] to [[Cheonan station|Cheonan]] are opened as another southward extension. :December 16: The Gyeongwon Line branch from [[Yongsan station]] to [[Hoegi station]] and the [[Jungang Line]] from [[Hoegi station]] to [[Deokso station]] open and are renamed to the separate '''Yongsan-Deokso Line'''. This service was the predecessor to the current [[Jungang Line]]. :December 21: [[Dongmyo station]] is opened. :December 27: [[Sema station|Sema]] and [[Osan College station|Osan College]] stations are opened. 2006 :June 30: [[Jinwi station|Jinwi]] and [[Jije station|Jije]] stations are opened. :July 1: Garibong station is renamed to '''Gasan Digital Complex'''. :December 15: 8 stations from [[Soyosan station|Soyosan]] to [[Ganeung station|Uijeongbu Bukbu]] are opened as a northward extension. A [[spur line]] to [[Gwangmyeong station]] is created. Uijeongbu Bukbu station is renamed to '''Ganeung'''. 2007 :December 28: [[Deokgye station]] is opened. 2008 :December 15: 6 stations from [[Cheonan station|Cheonan]] to [[Sinchang station|Sinchang]] are opened as another southward extension. :December 29: Siheung station is renamed to '''Geumcheon-gu Office'''. 2010 :January 21: [[Dangjeong station]] is opened. :February 26: [[Seodongtan station]] is opened. 2015 :February 3: Gyeongin Line express trains start stopping at [[Gaebong station|Gaebong]] and [[Jemulpo station|Jemulpo]] stations. 2017 :July 6: A new [[Limited express|limited express service]] was launched between [[Yongsan station|Yongsan]] and [[Dongincheon station|Dongincheon]] stations. 2019 :December 30: All Gyeongbu Line express trains start stopping at [[Geumjeong station|Geumjeong]] and [[Sungkyunkwan University station|Sungkyunkwan University]] stations and are extended to [[Cheongnyangni station|Cheongnyangni]]. Gyeongbu Line express trains no longer stop at [[Gunpo station]]. 2021 :October 30: [[Tangjeong station]] opened as an in-fill station between [[Asan station|Asan]] and [[Baebang station|Baebang]]. 2023 :December 16: Three stations from [[Soyosan station]] to [[Yeoncheon station]] on the Gyeongwon Line are opened as a northward extension of the line.{{Cite news |last=병극 |first=장 |date=14 December 2023 |title="전철 1호선의 진격...경원선 동두천-연천까지 달린다" |trans-title=“The advance of subway line 1... Gyeongwon Line runs from Dongducheon to Yeoncheon.” |work=Rail Economy News |url=https://www.redaily.co.kr/news/articleViewAmp.html?idxno=6967 |access-date=20 December 2023}} == Rolling stock == === Subway|Sinpo}} utINTACC~~{{small|K272}} {{stl|Seoul Metropolitan Subway|Incheon}} {{rint|seoul|1|size=15}} }} | map_state = collapsed }} The '''Suin–Bundang Line''' ({{Korean|hangul=수인·분당선}}; '''Su'''won–'''In'''cheon–'''Bundang''' Line) is a [[commuter rail]] service of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] system, operating on trackage from the [[Suin Line]] (opened on June 30, 2012) and the [[Bundang Line]] (opened on September 1, 1994). Operation began on September 12, 2020.{{cite web |date=17 September 2020 |title=돌아온 수인선, 하나로 이어진 수인분당선! |url=https://www.korea.kr/news/reporterView.do?newsId=148877748 |accessdate=12 January 2022 |publisher=대한민국 정책브리핑 Korea.kr |language=ko}} Frequent service is provided between [[Wangsimni Station|Wangsimni]] and [[Incheon Station|Incheon]] by 6-car trains, with 9 trains on weekdays and 5 on weekends running one station north of Wangsimni to [[Cheongnyangni Station|Cheongnyangni]]. However, many trains terminate at various locations on the line such as Jukjeon, Gosaek, and Oido. Trains travel along the Suin (Incheon-Suwon), Bundang (Suwon-Wangsimni), and Gyeongwon (Wangsimni-Cheongnyangni) lines. It shares trackage with the Ansan Line of [[Seoul Subway Line 4]] between Hanyang University at Ansan and Oido. Like all other Korail-run Seoul Metropolitan Subway lines, trains on the line run on the left-hand side of the track. The color shown on the map is [[Yellow (color)|yellow]]. == History == {| |- valign=top | 1994 || September 1 || The [[Bundang Line]] section is officially opened from [[Suseo station|Suseo]] to [[Ori station|Ori]] as the '''Bundang Line'''. |- valign=top | 2003 || September 3 || The Bundang Line section is extended northward from Suseo to [[Seolleung station|Seolleung]]. |- valign=top |rowspan=3| 2004 || January 16 || [[Imae station]] opens as an in-fill station on the Bundang Line section. |- valign=top | {{nowrap|September 24}} || [[Guryong station]] opens as an in-fill station on the Bundang Line section. |- valign=top | November 26 || A temporary [[Bojeong station]] opens on the Bundang Line section. |- valign=top | 2007 || December 24 || [[Jukjeon station]] opens as an in-fill station on the Bundang Line section. |- valign=top | 2011 || December 28 || The Bundang Line section is extended southward from Jukjeon to [[Giheung station|Giheung]]. The temporary Bojeong station is replaced with a new underground station, also called Bojeong.{{cite news| url=http://kojects.com/2011/11/13/date-released-for-southern-bundang-extension| title=Date Announced For Southern Bundang Extension| date=November 13, 2011| work=Kojects| access-date=December 15, 2011}} |- valign=top |rowspan=3| 2012 || June 30 || The [[Suin Line]] section is officially opened from [[Oido station|Oido]] to [[Songdo station|Songdo]] as the '''Suin Line'''. |- valign=top | October 6 || The Bundang Line section line is extended northward from Seolleung to [[Wangsimni station|Wangsimni]]. |- valign=top | December 1 || The Bundang Line section line is extended southward from Giheung to [[Mangpo station|Mangpo]]. |- valign=top | 2013 || November 30 || The Bundang Line section is extended westward from Mangpo to [[Suwon station|Suwon]], allowing for connections to Line 1. Express service is launched on the Bundang Line section. |- valign=top | 2014 || December 27 || [[Darwol station]] opens as an in-fill station on the Suin Line section. |- valign=top | 2016 || February 27 || The Suin Line section is extended westward from Songdo to [[Incheon station|Incheon]].{{cite web|url=http://www.kgnews.co.kr/news/articleView.html?idxno=264502|script-title=ko:경기신문 {{!}}{{!}} 수인선 전철 안산 구간 29일 착공|trans-title=Gyeonggi Newspaper {{!}}{{!}} Construction of the Ansan section of the Suin Line began on the 29th|work=Gyeonggi News|access-date=2011-05-01}} |- valign=top | 2017 || July 10 || Express service is launched on the Suin Line section. |- valign=top | 2018 || December 31 || The Bundang Line section is extended northeastward to [[Cheongnyangni station]], allowing for connections to the [[Gyeongchun Line]], Line 1, and regional rail services. |- valign=top | 2020 || September 12 || The Suin Line section is extended eastward from Oido to Suwon, and the present '''Suin-Bundang Line''' is formed when the Suin and Bundang lines are merged.{{cite magazine |first=David |last=Burroughs |url=https://www.railjournal.com/passenger/metros/final-phase-of-koreas-suin-line-complete/ |title=Final phase of Korea's Suin Line complete |magazine=[[International Railway Journal]] |publisher=Simmons-Boardman Publishing Inc. |date=15 September 2020 |access-date=2020-09-17}} |} ===Future plans=== Hagik station is planned to open between Songdo and [[Inha University station|Inha University]] in 2026. In addition, a connection line is under consideration at [[Suwon]] that will allow [[Korea Train eXpress|KTX]] trains to run between the [[Gyeongbu high-speed railway]] and [[Incheon Station]] via the Suin Line by 2025. == Rapid (Express) trains == Korail operates a variety of express "rapid" (급행) trains for [[regional rail|regional]] services on the Suin-Bundang Line during rush hours. These services include:
* Bundang Line express services, operating express between Gosaek (where they originate or terminate) and Jukjeon and then continuing as local trains to Wangsimni or Cheongnyangni. * Suin Line express services, operating express between Incheon and Oido (where they originate or terminate). == Stations == ===Current Routes=== *Wangsimni — Jukjeon/Gosaek/Incheon (most trains) *Cheongnyangni — Jukjeon/Gosaek (selected trains) *Oido — Incheon ('''S''' express) *Wangsimni — Gosaek ('''B''' express, Wangsimni — Jukjeon all stops) ===Stations=== '''''B''': Bundang Express Line''

Incheon Subway Line 2:
{{short description|Subway line in Incheon, South Korea}} {{Expand Korean|date=September 2022|topic=transp}} {{Infobox rail line | box_width = 22em | color = {{rcr|Incheon Subway|2}} | name = Incheon Line 2 | native_name = | logo = [[File:South Korea subway logo.svg|x40px]] [[File:Incheon Metro Line 2.svg|x40px]] | logo_alt = | image = [[File:Incheon Metro Class 2000 A01.jpg|265px]] | image_width = | image_alt = | caption = | type = [[Automatic train operation|Driverless]] [[Light metro]] | system = [[Incheon Subway]] | status = Operational | locale = | start = Geomdan Oryu | end = Unyeon | stations = 27 | routes = | daily_ridership = | open = {{start date and age|July 30, 2016}} | close = | owner = | operator = [[Incheon Transit Corporation]] | character = | depot = Unyeon | stock = | linelength = {{convert|29.2|km|abbr=on}} | tracklength = | tracks = 2 | gauge = | load_gauge = | minradius = | routenumber = | linenumber = | electrification = 750 V DC [[third rail]] | speed = | elevation = | website = | map = [[File:Incheon Metro Map en.svg|250px]] | map_state = collapsed }} '''Incheon Subway Line 2''' ({{Korean|hangul=인천도시철도 2호선}}) is a [[Automatic train operation|driverless]] [[light metro]] line running {{convert|29.2|km|mi|abbr=on}} from Oryu-dong in [[Seo-gu, Incheon|Seo-gu]] to Incheon Grand Park, with 27 stations,{{cite web|url=http://www.ictr.or.kr/eng/irtc/info.asp|title=General information|publisher=Incheon Transit Corporation|access-date=2017-01-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924032654/http://www.ictr.or.kr/eng/irtc/info.asp|archive-date=2015-09-24|url-status=dead}} part of the [[Incheon Subway]] system. The line is also included as a part of the overall [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] network; [[Juan station]] has a free transfer with [[Seoul Subway Line 1]], [[Geomam station]] connects with the [[AREX|AREX Line]] to [[Incheon International Airport]] and [[Seoul Station]], and Seongnam also has a free transfer with [[Seoul Subway Line 7]]. Line 2 has aboveground sections north of Asiad Stadium and east of Namdong-gu Office. ==History== * June 26, 2009: Construction begins. The price of building the line was 2 trillion [[South Korean won|KRW]]. * Early 2016: Trial runs begin. * July 30, 2016: The line opens, after seven years of construction.http://world.kbs.co.kr/english/news/news_Dm_detail.htm?No=120751| {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170510063006/http://world.kbs.co.kr/english/news/news_Dm_detail.htm?No=120751%7C |date=2017-05-10 }} title = Opening Ceremony Held for Incheon Subway Line No. 2{{Cite web | last =최 | first =미경 | title =인천시의회, 하루 만에 뒤집다 | publisher =Kiho News | date = June 22, 2012 | url =http://www.kihoilbo.co.kr/news/articleView.html?idxno=469721 | language = ko | access-date = 2012-06-25}} Line 2 was planned to open in August 2014, but the opening date was pushed back to July 30, 2016. ==Rolling stock and signaling== The ITC Line2 is a driverless, fully automatic subway system. The line uses 37 two-car trains, all of which were built in 2013 by [[Hyundai Rotem]], a member of [[Hyundai Motor Group]].{{cite journal|url=http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view//incheon-metro-line-2-signalling-contract-awarded.html|title=Incheon metro Line 2 signalling contract awarded|journal=[[Railway Gazette International]]|date=February 9, 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715161820/http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view/incheon-metro-line-2-signalling-contract-awarded.html|archive-date=July 15, 2011}}{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20110711164738/http://imrt2.hiconst.com/sub02_01.html The specification of the cars of Incheon Subway Line 2]}} They are similar to the [[Canada Line]] cars in [[Vancouver]], [[Canada]], albeit using the narrow light metro body ({{Cvt|2.65|m|ftin}}) and external sliding doors as opposed to the heavy metro wide body {{Cvt|3|m|ftin}} and pocket doors, and using a bottom contact third rail instead of the North American top contact. Currently, all stations are long enough to fit four-car trains (two sets mated together); however, current operations use two-car trains (one set per train). [[SelTrac]] [[Communications-based train control|CBTC]] is currently being implemented on the line by [[Thales Group|Thales]], along with an urban rail traffic management system; these systems will allow the trains to be driverless. ==Stations== All stations are in Incheon. {{Routemap |title = Incheon Subway Line 2 |title-bg = #{{rcr|Incheon Subway|2}} |collapse = yes |map = ^인천_국제공항_철@도 |hanja= 仁川國際空港鐵道 }} '''AREX''' ('''A'''irport '''R'''ailroad '''Ex'''press; {{Korean|hangul=인천국제공항철도}}) is an [[airport rail link]] and [[commuter rail]] line in the [[Seoul Metropolitan Area]] of South Korea, linking [[Incheon International Airport]] and [[Gimpo International Airport station|Gimpo International Airport]] with [[Seoul Station]]. The section between the two airports opened on March 23, 2007, and the line was extended to Seoul Station on December 29, 2010.The color used for route information for regular trains is a shade of blue called 'sea blue', while for express trains, it is orange. Long-distance [[Korea Train Express]] [[High-speed rail|high speed]] trains started to use the line from June 30, 2014, but discontinued the service in March 2018 due to low ridership. == History == === Phase 1 === The line was initially announced in July 1998 as the Incheon International Airport Railroad ({{Korean|hangul=인천국제공항철도|labels=no}}), abbreviated '''IREX''' ('''I'''ncheon Airport '''R'''ailroad '''Ex'''press) which can be seen on the railings of overhead crossings by the line.{{cite journal |last=Knutton |first=Mike |url=http://www.highbeam.com/doc/1G1-117667605.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100108010734/http://www.highbeam.com/doc/1G1-117667605.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=2010-01-08 |title=Korea's first airport railway to open in 2007 |date=May 2004 |journal=[[International Railway Journal]] |access-date=2010-12-30}} The project was launched as South Korea's first [[build-operate-transfer]] (BOT) franchise. The concession was won by a consortium of 11 Korean companies, which incorporated as the Incheon International Airport Railroad Company (IIARCo) in March 2001. The original main shareholders were [[Hyundai Group|Hyundai]] (27%), [[POSCO]] (11.9%), [[Daelim]] (10%), [[Dongbu Group|Dongbu]] (10%) and the [[Korail#History|Korean National Railroad]] (9.9%). After significant delays, construction of the line started in 2001,{{cite web |title=Seoul's airport rail link takes off |url=http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view//seouls-airport-rail-link-takes-off.html |publisher=[[Railway Gazette International]] |date=2007-05-01 |author=Chris Jackson |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110514143821/http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view/seouls-airport-rail-link-takes-off.html |archive-date=2011-05-14 }} after the opening of Incheon Airport. About 60% of the line is underground, and reaches Incheon International Airport on [[Yeongjong Island]] via the lower deck of [[Yeongjong Bridge]], a combined road-rail bridge. IIARCo contracted project management and the supply of equipment and trains, altogether worth about [[Euro|€]]400 million, to Incheon Korean French Consortium (IKFC). This consortium includes French company [[Alstom]], its South Korean subsidiary Eukorail, and the South Korean rolling stock manufacturing company [[Hyundai Rotem|Rotem]], which also supplied the technology for the [[Korea Train Express|KTX]] high-speed rail system. The line and the operating company was renamed AREX in June 2006. The first {{convert|37.6|km|mi|abbr=on}} segment from [[Incheon International Airport Terminal 1 station|Incheon International Airport Terminal 1]] to [[Gimpo International Airport station|Gimpo International Airport]] opened on March 23, 2007.{{cite web |title=Incheon-Gimpo Airports Train to Open Friday |url=http://english.chosun.com/site/data/html_dir/2007/03/21/2007032161019.html |publisher=[[The Chosun Ilbo]] |date=2007-03-21 |access-date=2014-08-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140826115720/http://english.chosun.com/site/data/html_dir/2007/03/21/2007032161019.html |archive-date=2014-08-26 |url-status=dead }} This makes AREX the first dedicated airport rail link and the second railway line in the Korean Peninsula (after [[Seoul Subway Line 5]]) to directly serve an airport. In March 2009, [[Korail]] acquired an 88.8% share of the company.{{cite web| url=http://www.hani.co.kr/arti/economy/economy_general/347119.html| script-title=ko:수천억 적자 공항철도, 코레일서 인수| language=ko| trans-title=Hundreds of billions of deficits acquired Airport Railroad, KORAIL| website=[[The Hankyoreh]]| date=2009-03-30| access-date=2013-07-12}} Upon the acquisition, the operating company changed its name to Korail Airport Railroad as of November 30, 2009. === Phase 2 === Phase 2 of the line runs from Gimpo International Airport to [[Seoul Station]], which increased the length to {{convert|58|km|mi|abbr=on}}.{{cite news |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/travel/news-and-advice/south-korea-to-open-airport-railway-next-month-2128227.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109201124/http://www.independent.co.uk/travel/news-and-advice/south-korea-to-open-airport-railway-next-month-2128227.html |archive-date=2012-11-09 |url-access=limited |url-status=live |title=South Korea to open airport railway next month |date=2010-11-08 |work=[[The Independent]] |access-date=2010-12-30}} At Gimpo the line is underground, rising to the surface to cross the [[Han River (Korea)|Han River]] on the [[Magok Railway Bridge]] before descending underground for the final time. This section was built in a cut and cover tunnel running parallel to [[Susaek station|Susaek]] and [[Gajwa station|Gajwa]] before connecting with [[Hongik University station|Hongik University]] and [[Gongdeok station|Gongdeok]], then diving to terminate deep underground on the west side of [[Seoul Station]]. The second phase opened for regular service on December 29, 2010,{{cite web |url=http://english.donga.com/srv/service.php3?biid=2010123087848 |title=To the airport in just 43 minutes |date=2010-12-30 |publisher=[[The Dong-A Ilbo]] |access-date=2010-12-30}} with the exception of Gongdeok station, which was planned to open around early 2011,{{cite web |url=http://english.chosun.com/site/data/html_dir/2010/12/29/2010122900856.html |title=Railway Linking Seoul Station and Incheon Airport Opens |date=2010-12-29 |publisher=[[The Chosun Ilbo]] |access-date=2010-12-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110101052538/http://english.chosun.com/site/data/html_dir/2010/12/29/2010122900856.html |archive-date=2011-01-01 |url-status=dead }} but was delayed to November 29, 2011.{{cite web|title=Main Arex Webpage |publisher=[[Korail]] |url=http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/main.jsp |format=webpage |access-date=2011-10-03 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110930170839/http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/main.jsp |archive-date=2011-09-30 }} === Phase 3 === Phase 3 of the line was opened in September 2018. During phase 3 Magongnaru station was opened. === Further extensions and improvements === [[File:Q707519 Yongyu station A01.jpg|thumb|[[AREX Class 2000|AREX 2000 series]] EMU at Yongyu Station in 2011, in use as temporary base station]] The city of [[Incheon]] requested that AREX add three new stations at [[Cheongna International City station|Cheongna International City]], [[Yeongjong station|Yeongjong]], and [[Yongyu station|Yongyu]]. The stations were originally planned to open with the second phase of the line, but were delayed due to delays in the [[real estate development]] projects the stations were to serve.{{cite web |url=http://news.donga.com/fbin/output?f=b0_&n=200907020290 |script-title=ko:공항철도 3개역 추가건설 연기 |language=ko |trans-title=Postponement of additional construction of 3 airport railway stations |date=2009-07-02 |publisher=[[The Dong-A Ilbo]] |access-date=2011-01-06 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130123130047/http://news.donga.com/fbin/output?f=b0_&n=200907020290 |archive-date=2013-01-23 }} Cheongna International City station, located west of Geomam was opened on June 21, 2014.{{cite web|url=http://www.tbs.seoul.kr/news/bunya.do?method=daum_html&typ_800=P&seq_800=10030500| title=Airport Express Line's Cheongna Station Opens| website=TBS eFM News| date=2014-06-21| first=Sunny| last=Chung}}{{cite web |url=http://kojects.com/2012/09/11/new-station-arex/#more-828| title=New Station to be added to Airport Railroad| first=Andy| last=Tebay| date=2012-09-11 |publisher=Kojects |access-date=2013-10-03}} Yeongjong station, located near the eastern part of the airport island, opened on March 26, 2016. The station was constructed as a station shell with two side platforms only, but underwent a conversion to fit in two outer tracks on each platform to allow for bypass moves. Earlier plans to link [[Yongsan station]] have been dropped. However, [[KTX]] service began to run from Yongsan Station to [[Incheon International Airport]] on June 30, 2014. These trains stop at two stations along the line: [[Geomam station|Geomam]] & [[Incheon International Airport Terminal 1 station|Incheon International Airport]].{{cite web| url=http://media.daum.net/foreign/englishnews/newsview?newsid=20140630193707124| title=KTX begins service to Incheon airport| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305071328/http://media.daum.net/foreign/englishnews/newsview?newsid=20140630193707124| archive-date=March 5, 2016}}{{cite web |url=http://www.korea.net/NewsFocus/Society/view?articleId=104880| title=KTX takes you from Seoul directly to Incheon Airport| date=2013-01-17 |website=[[Korea.net]]| access-date=2013-10-03| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131005021424/http://www.korea.net/NewsFocus/Society/view?articleId=104880| archive-date=October 5, 2013}} In March 2018, KTX service to Incheon Airport was suspended due to low ridership.{{cite web |url=https://kojects.com/2018/06/10/incheon-airport-ktx-suspended/ |title=Incheon Airport KTX Service Suspended |last=Medimorec |first=Nikola |date=10 June 2018 |website=Kojects |access-date=9 July 2018 |quote=In 2014, KORAIL began to offer KTX high-speed train connections from Incheon International Airport to Seoul, Daegu, Busan and other cities in Korea. The Incheon Airport KTX service was suspended now at the end of March 2018. The main reason was that the demand was too low and ridership stayed far below necessary numbers.}} The suspension became permanent in September 2018 as the line was officially abolished.{{cite web |url=http://www.fntoday.co.kr/news/articleView.html?idxno=167285 |script-title=ko:9월부터 인천공항 KTX 노선 폐지| trans-title=Incheon Airport KTX route abolished from September |last=박 |first=재균 |date=7 August 2018 |website=FNToday |access-date=10 August 2018 |language=ko}}{{cite web |url=http://news1.kr/photos/details/?3242951 |script-title=ko:2014년 개통한 '인천공항 KTX' 폐지| trans-title='Incheon Airport KTX', which opened in 2014, was abolished| last=민 |first=경석 |date=7 August 2018 |website=News1 |access-date=10 August 2018 |language=ko}} On September 1, 2010, the South Korean government announced a strategic plan to reduce travel times from Seoul to 95% of the country to under 2 hours by 2020. As part of the plan, the AREX line is to be further upgraded to handle {{convert|230|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}} speed limits.{{cite web |url=http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2925474 |title=Bullet trains coming to a town near you by 2020 |date=2010-09-02 |publisher=[[JoongAng Daily]] |access-date=2010-10-27| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20101106102918/http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2925474| archive-date= 6 November 2010 | url-status= live}} [[Incheon International Airport]] opened Terminal 2 on January 18, 2018, though the AREX line was extended to the terminal's station 5 days earlier. This brings the whole line to a length of {{convert|63.8|km|abbr=on}}.{{cite magazine| magazine=BLICKPUNKT STRASSENBAHN| date=February 2018| page=135| title=Kurzmeldungen}} There is currently{{update inline|date=September 2023}} a proposal for [[Seoul Subway Line 9]] to combine with the AREX line via a direct connection at the Gimpo International Airport Station starting in 2020, providing a through service from Incheon International Airport to the [[Gangnam]] area with a travel time of about one hour.{{cite web| url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20150526000934| title=Incheon airport subway links to be extended| website=[[The Korea Herald]]| last=Lee| first=Hyun-jeong| date=26 May 2015}} == Rolling stock == [[File:Interior of A'REX commuter train.jpg|thumb|Interior of an [[AREX Class 2000|AREX 2000 series]] EMU]] [[File:Korail A'rex Direct Train Interior.jpg|thumb|Interior of an AREX 1000 series EMU]] {| class="wikitable" |+ !Name !Built !Manufacturer !Number built !Notes |- |AREX Class 1000 |2006, 2009 | rowspan="3" |[[Hyundai Rotem]] |6 |Used on express services |- |AREX Class 2000 (1st generation) |2005–2006 2009-2010, 2017 |22 | rowspan="2" |Used on commuter services |- |AREX Class 2000 (2nd generation) |2025 |9 |} AREX operates two types of trains, one for commuter and one for express services, both supplied by [[Hyundai Rotem]]. Three 1000 series express trains and nine [[AREX Class 2000|2000 series]] commuter trains were delivered for phase 1 of the project, and original plans foresaw a doubling of their numbers for the second phase. Both trains are six-car [[electric multiple unit]]s, which can be expanded to eight cars in the future. End cars and the fourth car are trailers, the second, third and fifth car are powered.{{cite web |url=http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/advert/train.jsp |script-title=ko:일반ㆍ직통열차 | trans-title=General·Direct Train |language=ko |publisher=AREX |access-date=2010-12-30 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101230031841/http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/advert/train.jsp |archive-date=2010-12-30}} The trains have a design speed of {{convert|120|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}}, but are operated at a maximum {{convert|110|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}} on surface tracks and a maximum {{convert|100|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}} in tunnels. Each car has a length of {{convert|19.95|m|ftin|abbr=on}} and a width of {{convert|3.12|m|ftin|abbr=on}}, with a carbody made of [[aluminium]]. The commuter version has four pairs of sliding doors on each side of each car, the express version has two pairs of sliding doors on each side of each car. Express trains have 272 high-back forward and rear-facing seats with armrests in a 2+2 configuration, as well as overhead luggage shelves, additional luggage racks, an onboard restroom, and complimentary WiFi.{{cite web| script-title=ko:열차소개| language=ko| trans-title=Express Train Introduction |url=https://www.arex.or.kr/content.do?url=&menuNo=MN201503300000000001 |publisher=AREX |access-date=2018-01-05}} The transition between cars are separated by transparent sliding doors. Commuter trains are similar to regular metro trains with seats along the walls but do not have overhead shelves. These are [[open gangway]] trains, with no separating doors between cars. Commuter trains offer seating for 282 passengers and standing room for 630 passengers. Both train types offer disabled seats and are equipped with LCD screens for passenger information, including flight arrivals and departures. On September 15, 2025, the first of nine additional 2000 series trains began revenue service on the all-stop service.{{Cite web |last=Kang |first=Jun-wan |date=2025-09-15 |title=공항철도, 시속 150km 신규 전동차 오늘 운행 |url=https://www.hankyung.com/article/202509142351h |access-date=2025-09-17 |website=[[The Korea Economic Daily|Hankyung]] |language=ko}} The remaining eight sets are expected to be in service by the end of the year. File:AREX-Train-Mockup-2123-Exterior.jpg|Mockup of a 2000 series car with updated design == Operation == === Services === [[File:AREX-Incheon-International-Airport-Station-Gates.JPG|thumb|Ticketing gate, AREX Incheon International Airport station]] [[File:Incheonairport-arexstation-jpgver.jpg|thumb|250px|View of the Terminal 1 station at Incheon International Airport.]] Both non-stop Express and all-stops commuter services are operated. With the opening of the first phase, journeys from Incheon International Airport to Gimpo International Airport took 28 minutes on express services and 33 minutes on commuter services. Following the opening of the second phase, Seoul–Incheon International Airport travel time on half-hourly Express services is 43 minutes to Terminal 1. The all-stops commuter service, which runs at a frequency of up to one train every 6 minutes, takes 53 minutes. This compares with an average travel time of 70 minutes on buses and 60 minutes in taxis or private cars. Express train passengers boarding certain airlines can check in to their flights at the [[Seoul Station City Airport Terminal]] on the western side of [[Seoul Station]],{{cite web |url=http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2930308 |title=On opening day, airport express trip's a breeze |date=2010-12-30 |publisher=[[JoongAng Daily]] |access-date=2010-12-30| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110119184118/http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2930308| archive-date= 19 January 2011 | url-status= live}} which include facilities to check in and x-ray baggage, receive exit stamps from an onsite Korean Immigration Service officer, and permit expedited security screening once at Incheon.{{cite web| script-title=ko:도심공항터미널 이용개요| language=ko| trans-title=City Airport Terminal Use Information |url=https://www.arex.or.kr/content.do?url=&menuNo=MN201503300000000026 |publisher=AREX |access-date=2018-01-05}} The AREX line is shown on Seoul subway maps as sky blue, often bisected with orange striping. AREX was originally not considered part of the subway network, but following the majority buyout by [[Korail]], who operates many of Seoul's subway lines, the commuter line was fully integrated with the rest of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] system. There are now free transfers for all Seoul and [[Incheon Subway|Incheon metro lines]] it crosses paths with. ==== AREX Express trains ==== AREX Express train stations and ticket windows are easily by their distinct orange color scheme. AREX Express trains offer 2+2 configured reclining seating, ample luggage storage, free WiFi, onboard service attendants and electronic entertainment screens. AREX Express passengers have access to connecting shuttle buses at Seoul Station to partner hotels, to a dedicated boarding lounge at Incheon Airport Terminal 1, and exclusive use of Seoul Station's City Airport Terminal when departing. All seats on the AREX Express trains are reserved, with receipts displaying the assigned car and seat numbers for passengers alongside their tickets.{{Cite web|title=AREX Express {{!}} Seoul - Incheon Airport Train {{!}} Reviews, Photos & More|url=https://trainreview.com/train/arex-express|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201012084654/https://trainreview.com/train/arex-express|url-status=usurped|archive-date=October 12, 2020|access-date=2020-10-12|website=trainreview.com}} ==== Gimpo International Airport ==== AREX-Express trains do not stop at Gimpo International Airport. To access Gimpo International Airport from both Seoul Station and Incheon International Airport, passengers need to travel on an all-stops train.{{Cite web|title=AREX All Stop {{!}} Seoul - Incheon Airport Train {{!}} Reviews, Photos & More|url=https://trainreview.com/train/arex-all-stop|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210413194014/https://trainreview.com/train/arex-all-stop|url-status=usurped|archive-date=April 13, 2021|access-date=2020-10-12|website=trainreview.com}} ==== KTX ==== The opening of the second phase in 2010 allowed passengers to connect directly from KTX to AREX services at Seoul Station, rather than requiring a subway connection at Gimpo. In November 2013, the AREX-[[Gyeongui Line]] direct connection was completed, and on June 30, 2014, KTX trains started full service on the AREX line to Incheon.{{cite web| url=http://english.visitkorea.or.kr/enu/FU/FU_EN_15.jsp?cid=1930476| title=KTX trains now directly connect to Incheon Int'l Airport| website=[[VisitKorea.or.kr]]| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151115002910/http://english.visitkorea.or.kr/enu/FU/FU_EN_15.jsp?cid=1930476| archive-date=November 15, 2015}}{{cite web| url=http://www.koreaherald.com/view.php?ud=20140630001212| title=KTX begins service to Incheon airport| website=[[The Korea Herald]]| date=30 June 2014}}{{cite web| url=http://news.mk.co.kr/newsRead.php?year=2014&no=942367| script-title=ko:인천공항까지 KTX로 직행…환승없이 운행| language=ko| trans-title=Directly to Incheon Airport by KTX... Operates without transfer| date=2014-06-30}}{{cite web| url=http://www.ajunews.com/view/20140616105744964| title=KTX 인천국제공항역 개통, 부산·광주~인천공항 KTX로 다닌다| language=ko| trans-title=Opening of KTX Incheon International Airport Station, going to Busan Gwangju-Incheon Airport KTX| date=2014-06-16}} The expected travel time at construction was 2 hours 41 minutes to [[Busan station|Busan]] and 3 hours to [[Gwangju station|Gwangju]], but as of 2015, actual travel times were roughly 3 hours to Gwangju and slightly over 3.5 to Busan.{{cite web| url=http://www.electimes.com/home/news/main/viewmain.jsp?news_uid=78391 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120301224526/http://www.electimes.com/home/news/main/viewmain.jsp?news_uid=78391 |url-status=dead |archive-date=2012-03-01 |script-title=ko:공항철도, KTX, 지하철 9호선과 직결 활성화 시킨다 | trans-title=Directly connected to Airport Railroad, KTX, and Subway Line 9 to activate| language=ko |date=2010-09-30 |publisher=The Korea Electric Times |access-date=2010-10-30 }}{{cite web| url=http://kojects.com/incheon-airport-ktx/| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160324141015/http://kojects.com/incheon-airport-ktx/| archive-date=March 24, 2016| title=Incheon Airport KTX Schedules| date=2015-06-27| website=Kojects| access-date=2016-10-10}} In late March 2018, KTX service to and from the airport was suspended because of low ridership{{Cite web| url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/ShowTopic-g294196-i8160-k11444229-No_KTX_at_Incheon_Airport-South_Korea.html| title=No KTX at Incheon Airport| website=TripAdvisor| date=2018}}{{User-generated inline| date=September 2020| certain=yes}} and the suspension became permanent in September 2018 as the line was officially abolished. At the time of the [[2018 Winter Olympics]], the Korean [[Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport (South Korea)|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport]] reported at 15% demand on weekdays. === Fares === [[File:Arex-express-ticket-southkorea.png|thumb|AREX Express ticket]] After the opening of the first phase, in 2007, a ticket for a ride from Gimpo International Airport to Incheon International Airport cost [[South Korean Won|₩]]3,100, both on commuter and express services.{{cite web |title=Fare |url=http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/fare/fare.jsp |publisher=AREX |access-date=2010-12-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070625054410/http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/fare/fare.jsp |archive-date=2007-06-25}} This fare increased in steps to ₩3,500 by the end of 2010,{{cite web |url=http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/board/news_list.jsp?num=260&mode=view |script-title=ko:코레일공항철도 운임100원 인상 |trans-title=KORAIL Airport Railroad fare increase of 100 won |language=ko |date=2010-08-27 |publisher=AREX |access-date=2010-12-30 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722141253/http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/board/news_list.jsp?num=260&mode=view |archive-date=2011-07-22 }} then was reduced to ₩3,300 for commuter trains when the second phase opened.{{cite web|title=Fare |url=http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/fare/fare.jsp |publisher=AREX |access-date=2010-12-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101114142635/http://www.arex.or.kr/jsp/eng/fare/fare.jsp |archive-date=14 November 2010 |url-status=dead }} The fare for express services, which travel the full Seoul–Incheon International Airport distance non-stop from the opening of the second phase in December 2010, was set at ₩13,300; while the fare for commuter services on the full distance was set at ₩3,700. This competes against a price of around ₩10,000 on buses, ₩67,500 on taxis, and about ₩15,500 with private car. By 2022, the fares for the full length of the AREX line settled on ₩4,750 for commuter trains,{{cite web |title=Fare Information (All Stop) |url=https://www.arex.or.kr/content.do?url=&menuNo=MN201503300000000008 |publisher=AREX |access-date=2022-09-27}} and ₩9,500 for Express service{{cite web |title=Fare Information (Express Train) |url=https://www.arex.or.kr/content.do?url=&menuNo=MN201503300000000003 |publisher=AREX |access-date=2022-09-27}} and both require an additional ₩500 deposit if traveling by a single use card. Vending machines for local trains accept Korean Won only. Vending machines for Express train tickets take Korean Won and Korean credit cards. Passengers travelling with foreign credit cards must purchase their tickets from staffed ticket windows.{{Cite web|title=AREX All Stop {{!}} Seoul - Incheon Airport Train {{!}} Reviews, Photos & More|url=https://trainreview.com/train/arex-express|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201012084654/https://trainreview.com/train/arex-express|url-status=usurped|archive-date=October 12, 2020|access-date=2020-10-12|website=trainreview.com}} ===Ridership=== After the opening of the first phase, ridership was initially been far below expectations. Actual ridership in 2008 was around 16,000 passengers/day, or only around 7% of the forecast 230,000, necessitating subsidies of ₩166 billion.{{cite web| url=https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/www/news/opinon/2009/09/137_51601.html| title=Opinion: Waste of Taxpayer Money| website=[[The Korea Times]]| date=2009-09-10| access-date=2013-07-12}} Hyundai had originally forecast ridership of up to 490,000/day when the link to Seoul Station is completed. However, ridership rose significantly after the start of service on the second phase of the line to Seoul Station. As early as the first week, average daily ridership more than doubled, from 27,210 to 58,000.{{cite web |url=http://news.korail.com/main/php/search_view.php?idx=18893 |script-title=ko:서울역 '도심속 공항' 탈바꿈 |language=ko |date=2011-01-13 |publisher=[[Korail]] |access-date=2011-01-18 |trans-title=Seoul Station transformed into a "city center airport"| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120304014926/http://news.korail.com/main/php/search_view.php?idx=18893 |archive-date=2012-03-04 |url-status=dead }} {{As of|September 2013}}, the all-stops commuter service has approximately 150,000 passengers per day, while the non-stop express service sees only around 2,000 passengers.{{cite web| url=http://mengnews.joins.com/view.aspx?aId=2977527| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161022095309/http://mengnews.joins.com/view.aspx?aId=2977527| archive-date=October 22, 2016| title=Seoul Station is a hit for air travel| website=[[Korea JoongAng Daily]]| date=September 13, 2013}} == Stations == {| class="wikitable" |- !colspan="2"|Station !colspan="2" |Stopping pattern !rowspan="2" width=210|Connecting services !colspan="2"|Distance !colspan="2" rowspan="2"|Location |- !# !Name

December 21, 2010 (realigned route) | close = December 20, 2010 (original route) | owner = [[Korea Rail Network Authority]] | operator = [[Korail]] | character = | depot = | stock = [[Korail Class 361000|Class 361000]], [[Korail Class 368000|ITX-Cheongchun]], Mugunghwa-ho | tracklength = {{convert|80.7|km|abbr=on}} | tracks = [[Double track]] | gauge = {{track gauge|sg}} | load_gauge = | minradius = | routenumber = | linenumber = | electrification = 25 kV/60 Hz [[Alternating Current|AC]] [[Overhead wires|catenary]] | speed = {{convert|180|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}} (ITX trains) / {{convert|110|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}} (commuter trains) | elevation_m = | elevation = | website = | map = }} The '''Gyeongchun Line''' is a [[regional rail]] line between [[Seoul]] and [[Chuncheon]], [[South Korea]], operated by [[Korail]]. Its name is derived from '''''Gyeong'''seong'' ({{lang|ko|京}}, meaning the capital, Seoul) and '''''Chun'''cheon''. It was completely reconstructed in the 2000s. Service on it has operated between [[Sangbong station]] on the [[Jungang Line]] in eastern Seoul and [[Chuncheon station]], as part of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] system, since December 21, 2010. A class of regional rail service named '''[[ITX-Cheongchun]]''' began operations on February 28, 2012, linking Chuncheon to [[Cheongnyangni station|Cheongnyangni]] and [[Yongsan station|Yongsan]] Stations. == History == The original Gyeongchun Line was opened along its full length of {{convert|87.3|km|abbr=on}} between [[Kwangwoon University station|Kwangwoon University]] on the [[Gyeongwon Line]] to Chuncheon by the privately owned [[Gyeongchun Railway]] on 20 July 1939.{{cite web |url=http://info.korail.com/2007/kra/ope/ope08000/w_ope08606.jsp |title=경영원칙 - 경영공시 - 영업현황 - 영업거리현황 |publisher=[[Korail]] |access-date=2010-12-04 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722192217/http://info.korail.com/2007/kra/ope/ope08000/w_ope08606.jsp |archive-date=2011-07-22 }}私設鉄道運輸開始, 朝鮮総督府官報(The Public Journal of the Governor-General of Korea)　Showa Nr. 3754, 26 July 1939 Chuncheon was the most popular destination for students on orientation trips, bringing passengers to the line. Following the [[Liberation of Korea]], all railways, including the Gyeongchun Railway, were nationalised. === Upgrade === The line was upgraded into an electrified and double-tracked line for {{convert|180|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.railjournal.com/altfeature1.shtml |title=Korea's railways face a bright future |date=2008-07-01 |publisher=[[International Railway Journal]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090416195911/http://www.railjournal.com/altfeature1.shtml |archive-date=2009-04-16 |access-date=2010-08-29 |url-status=dead }}{{cite web |url=http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view/10/south-koreas-growing-network.html |title=South Korea's growing network |date=2008-09-08 |publisher=[[Railway Gazette International]] |access-date=2010-08-29 |archive-date=2012-12-05 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121205072403/http://www.railwaygazette.com/news/single-view/view/10/south-koreas-growing-network.html |url-status=dead }} Between Geumgok and Chuncheon, from 1997 until 2010, the line was re-laid in a straighter, 64.2 km long alignment with a budget of 2.151,931 billion won.{{cite web |url=http://www.krnetwork.or.kr/service/condition/RouteMapView.do?PA_MAP_ID=12373 |title=경춘선 복선전철 |publisher=Korea Rail Network Authority |access-date=2010-10-24}} The remaining 17.9 km of the upgraded line was built with a separate budget of 574.124 billion won.{{cite web |url=http://www.krnetwork.or.kr/service/condition/RouteMapView.do?PA_MAP_ID=12426 |title=망우~금곡 복선전철 |publisher=Korea Rail Network Authority |access-date=2010-10-24}} Towards Seoul, after [[Toegyewon station]], this section of the new line diverges from the old alignment that ended in Seongbuk, and connects to the [[Jungang Line]] at [[Mangu station]]. The new alignment was originally planned to be opened in 2004, but completion of the works was delayed for various reasons, including lack of funds. The complete new alignment opened and the old one closed on December 21, 2010.{{cite web |url=http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2929395 |title=Historic railway line chugs on into history |date=2010-12-09 |publisher=[[Korea JoongAng Daily]] |access-date=2010-12-16}} [[File:Re-alignment of Gyeongchun Line.gif|thumb|Upgrade of Gyeongchun Line
(Former alignment with red line and current alignment with blue line)|250px]] On September 1, 2010, the South Korean government announced a strategic plan to reduce travel times from Seoul to 95% of the country to under 2 hours by 2020. As part of the plan, the Gyeongchun Line is to be further upgraded for 230 km/h and may see [[Korea Train Express|KTX]] service.{{cite web |url=http://joongangdaily.joins.com/article/view.asp?aid=2925474 |title=Bullet trains coming to a town near you by 2020 |date=2010-09-02 |publisher=[[JoongAng Daily]] |access-date=2010-10-27}} For the longer term, the government also considers to build a parallel high-speed line that would continue beyond Chuncheon to [[Sokcho]] on South Korea's east coast. On November 4, 2016, two trains (one in the morning, one in the night) were added to the line. These trains run to and from [[Kwangwoon University station]], allowing for transfers to [[Seoul Subway Line 1|Line 1]]. These trains take the Mangu Line Branch. On September 26, 2016, the service was extended to [[Cheongnyangni station]] to improve access to regional trains (KTX, Seoul Metro Line 1) at the station. However, only 10 trains in each direction travel past Sangbong Station; the majority of the trains still terminate at Sangbong Station, and the two special rush-hour trains still run to and from Kwangwoon University Station.{{cite web |url=http://www.letskorail.com/etc/excel/160926_10_Sangbong-Chonchen.xls |title=Archived copy |access-date=2016-12-02 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170210210902/http://www.letskorail.com/etc/excel/160926_10_Sangbong-Chonchen.xls |archive-date=2017-02-10 }} == Services == === Seoul Metropolitan Subway Gyeongchun Line === {{Infobox rail service | box_width = 22em | name = Seoul Metropolitan Subway Gyeongchun Line | color = {{rcr|Seoul Metropolitan Subway|Gyeongchun}} | logo = [[File:South Korea subway logo.svg|x40px]] [[File:Gyeongchun Line.svg|x40px]] | logo_width = | image = [[File:Korail Class 361000 EMU.jpg|265px]] | image_width = | caption = Korail Class 361000 EMU | type = [[Passenger rail terminology#Heavy rail|Heavy rail]], Passenger rail

passenger service was integrated into the Seoul Metropolitan Subway system as a name of ''Seoul Metropolitan Subway Gyeongchun Line (수도권 전철 경춘선)''.{{cite news|last=Gisam|first=Hong|date=December 13, 2019|title=서울 지하철 6호선 신내역 21일 개통...경춘선과 만난다|trans-title=Seoul Metro Line 6 Sinnae station opens - connects Gyeongchun Line|url=https://www.news1.kr/articles/?3792506|language=ko|work=News1 News|location=Seoul|access-date=January 27, 2021|quote=서울 지하철 6호선이 신내역 개통을 통해 수도권 전철 경춘선과 만난다.}} It brings that the system from Seoul all the way into [[Gangwon Province, South Korea|Gangwon Province]]. The new service reduced travel time between Chuncheon and [[Sangbong station|Sangbong]] in Seoul from two hours to 89 minutes, with different trains operating according to different stopping patterns; and increased capacity five-fold. Compared to the previous [[Mugunghwa-ho]] train service on the Gyeongchun Line, fares were reduced by half. For the service, [[Hyundai Rotem]] supplied Korail with fifteen eight-car [[Korail Class 361000|Class 361000]] [[Electric multiple unit|EMU]] trains, out of which only fourteen trains remain in service on the line today. Currently, Seoul Metropolitan Subway Gyeongchun Line serves mainly Sangbong station to Chuncheon station. Rare service to Cheongnyangni or Kwangwoon University station is also available, making it a Y-shaped line. === ITX-Cheongchun service=== On February 28, 2012, Korail introduced the [[ITX-Cheongchun]] service ('''I'''ntercity '''T'''rain E'''X'''press), which uses Class 368000 trains with double-deck cars. From Chuncheon Station, the fastest ITX trains take 52 minutes to [[Cheongnyangni station]], and 68 minutes to [[Yongsan station]] in Seoul, operating at a maximum speed of {{convert|180|km/h|mph|0|abbr=on}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mRGcA84G318|title=[Full HD] 경춘선 좌석급행 2층열차 / Gyeongchun Line's new trains coming|last=loveaselin|date=9 September 2011|via=YouTube}} The base fare is 9,800 [[South Korean won|won]] between Chuncheon and Yongsan, but Korail offers 15% discount at all time resulting price of 8,300 won. Basic discount rate was 30% until July 31, 2016, 25% until July 31, 2018, 15% since August 1, 2018.{{cite news|last=Sanghak|first=Lee|date=July 13, 2018|title=경춘선'ITX-청춘'8월부터 요금 올라...춘천~용산 8천300원|trans-title=Fare of Gyeongchun Line 'ITX-Cheongchun' increases - 8,300 won for Chuncheon to Yongsan|url=https://www.yna.co.kr/view/AKR20180713122800062|language=ko|work=Yonhapnews|location=Seoul|access-date=January 27, 2021}} === Mugunghwa service === Some special Mugunghwa trains which deploy military troops takes the Gyeongchun Line.{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xCuHu5fSJFE|title= [철도] 경춘선 건설무궁화호 남춘천역 통과 영상 (19.02.21) |trans-title= [Railroad] Gyeongchun Line Gyeonseol Mugunghwa-ho train passes through Namchuncheon station(19.02.21)|publisher=PDC HANEUL TV|access-date=January 27, 2021|date=March 2, 2016|via=YouTube}} == Service Route == === Current Services === ==== Main Line ==== The following stations are along the [[Gyeongui-Jungang Line]] and the Gyeongchun Line itself. Shinbundang Line:
{{Short description|Driverless subway line in Gyeonggi-do and Seoul, South Korea}} {{Infobox rail line | box_width = 22em | color = {{rcr|Seoul Metropolitan Subway|Shinbundang}} | name = Shinbundang Line
(DX Line) | native_name = {{Lang|ko|신분당선(新盆唐線)}}
''Sinbundangseon'' | logo = [[File:South Korea subway logo.svg|x40px]] [[File:Seoul Metro Line Shinbundang Bilingual.svg|x40px]] | logo_alt = | image = [[File:Shinbundang Line D104.jpg|250px]] | image_width = | image_alt = | caption = Shinbundang line train | type = [[Commuter Rail]] | system = [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]] | status = Operational | locale = | start = [[Sinsa station|Sinsa]] | end = [[Gwanggyo station|Gwanggyo]] | stations = 16 | routes = | daily_ridership = | open = {{start date and age|28 October 2011}} | close = | owner = | operator = Shinbundang Railroad Corporation, Gyeonggi Railroad Co., Ltd., New Seoul Railroad Co., Ltd., [[Neo Trans]] | character = | depot = | stock = | linelength_km = | linelength = {{convert|33.4|km|abbr=on}} | tracklength_km = | tracklength = | tracks = 2 | gauge = {{Track gauge|sg|allk=on}} | load_gauge = | minradius = | routenumber = | linenumber = | electrification = 25 kV, 60 Hz AC | speed_km/h = | speed = | elevation_m = | elevation = | website = | map = | map_state = collapsed }} The '''Shinbundang Line''' ({{Korean|hangul=신분당선 |hanja=新盆唐線}}; literally, ''New [[Bundang Line]]'') or '''DX Line''' for Dynamic Express Line is a {{convert|33.4|km|abbr=on}} long line of the [[Seoul Metropolitan Subway]]. It is the world's fifth{{cite web |url= http://www.shinbundang.co.kr/index.jsp?pageID=/notice/notice2_view.jsp?no=612&open_main=3&open_sub1=2&open_sub2=0 |script-title= ko:보도자료 |website= www.shinbundang.co.kr |publisher= 전국매일 |last= 이 |first= 일영 |language= ko |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20131203004228/http://www.shinbundang.co.kr/index.jsp?pageID=%2Fnotice%2Fnotice2_view.jsp%3Fno%3D612&open_main=3&open_sub1=2&open_sub2=0 |archive-date= 3 December 2013 |access-date= 10 September 2018 |url-status= live }} subway to run completely driverless and the second completely driverless metro line to open in South Korea, after [[Busan Subway Line 4]]. It connects [[Sinsa station]] and [[Gwanggyo station]] in 42 minutes, a feat achieved by being the first line to operate South Korea's next-generation subway car travelling at over {{convert|90|km/h|abbr=on}}, with the fastest average speed of any subway line in the country.{{cite news|title=New Subway Line Shortens Bundang-Gangnam Trip |url= http://english.chosun.com/site/data/html_dir/2011/07/15/2011071500981.html |access-date= 25 April 2012 |newspaper=[[The Chosun Ilbo]] |date=15 July 2011}}{{cite web |url= http://www.hankookilbo.com/v/3cb3a5de161c44eb83805380de2b984d |last= 유 |first= 명식 |date= 12 January 2016 |language= ko |script-title=ko:신분당선 연장선 "빠른데도 소음이 없어요" |publisher=[[Hankook Ilbo]]}} The initial route opened in October 2011 passed through 6 stations intersecting with three existing lines at [[Gangnam station]] ([[Seoul Subway Line 2|Line 2]]), [[Yangjae station]] ([[Seoul Subway Line 3|Line 3]]), and [[Jeongja station]] ([[Bundang Line]]). Phase 2 opened on 30 January 2016 added 6 stations to the south, extending the line to serve [[Yongin]]'s [[Suji-gu]] and [[Suwon]]'s [[Gwanggyo New Town]]. Construction for Phase 3's extension northwards to [[Sinsa station]] started in 2016, with opening on 28 May 2022.{{cite news |last=Ilbo |first=Dong-A |date=May 28, 2022 |title=Shinbundang Line 'Gangnam~Shinsa' opens today... Gwanggyo → Shrine 42 minutes |url=https://www.donga.com/news/Economy/article/all/20220528/113665032/1 |location= Seoul, South Korea |access-date=May 28, 2022}} Construction of the line started in mid-2005, for revenue start in September 2011. However, because of the [[2011 Seoul floods|flooding of late July 2011]], the opening was delayed and eventually opened on 28 October 2011.{{cite news| url=http://teflorbust.wordpress.com/2011/08/01/sin-bundang-line-set-back-by-floods |last= Daugherty |first= Daniel | title=Sin Bundang Line Set Back by Floods| date=August 1, 2011 | work=맙소사! | access-date= 28 September 2011}}{{cite news| url=http://teflorbust.wordpress.com/2011/09/04/sin-bundang-tentative-opening-date-oct-28| title=New Sin Bundang Opening Date: Oct. 28| date=September 4, 2011 |last= Daugherty |first= Daniel | work=맙소사! | access-date= 28 September 2011}} Gwanggyo Jungang ([[Ajou University|Ajou Univ.]]) station opened its underground bus transfer station in early March 2016, where [[platform screen doors|screen doors]] are installed on bus platforms so bus riders from all over [[Suwon]] can transfer to the subway directly.{{citation needed|date=March 2025}} The price to ride the Shinbundang Line is higher than standard fare for the rest of the Seoul subway system. There are zone based surcharges in addition to regular fares which are identical to other lines.{{citation needed|date=March 2025}} == Phases == [[File:D07 Gangnam Station Transfer Gate.JPG|250px|thumb|Transfer gate to [[Seoul Subway Line 2]] at [[Gangnam station]]]] [[File:D07 Gangnam Station Terminal Platform.JPG|250px|thumb|[[Gangnam station]] platform]] [[File:Shinbundang-jeonmang 20111012.jpg|250px|thumb|The front of the subway car is open for viewing thanks to driverless operation.]] [[File:Shinbundang Line Class D000 EMU Interior.jpg|250px|thumb|The interior of the 2011 introduced subway car built by [[Hyundai Rotem]]]] Phase 1 alleviated some of the traffic from inner Seoul to Bundang which is mainly residential and within the [[Seoul Capital Area]]. Phase 1 was {{convert|17.3|km|abbr=on}} and intersects with several subway lines in south-eastern Seoul as well as with the Bundang Line. Travel from one end to the other was 16 minutes. Construction lasted for roughly six years starting in mid-2005 and ended at the end of October 2011. Total cost between public and private sectors was estimated at 1.169 trillion won.{{cite web| url=http://shinbundang.co.kr/information/information04.asp| script-title=ko:DX Line 홈페이지에 오신것을 환영합니다| publisher=DX LINE | language=ko | access-date=11 May 2011 | url-status=dead| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110827043623/http://shinbundang.co.kr/information/information04.asp |author=| archive-date=27 August 2011}}{{cite web| url=http://shinbundang.co.kr/information/information05.asp| script-title=ko:DX Line 홈페이지에 오신것을 환영합니다| publisher=DX LINE| language=ko| access-date=11 May 2011| url-status=dead| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110411024425/http://www.shinbundang.co.kr/information/information05.asp | archive-date=11 April 2011}}{{cite press release | url= http://www.industrykorea.net/BCS_Com/Project/Policy/Rotem/Rotem(08-05)/LinkData/080423-PPP_consortium(M).doc | title= Construction of Sin Bundang Line on Track for 2010 Launch | publisher= IndustryKorea.net | format= DOC | access-date= 11 May 2011 | archive-date= 21 March 2012 | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20120321204227/http://www.industrykorea.net/BCS_Com/Project/Policy/Rotem/Rotem(08-05)/LinkData/080423-PPP_consortium(M).doc | url-status= dead }} [[File:Gangnam Station, Shinbundang Line, Seoul, South Korea.jpg|250px|thumb|Platform of [[Gangnam station]]]] Phase 2, a southern extension of the line, was primarily designed to ease heavy traffic between [[Yongin]]'s [[Suji-gu]] and Bundang/Seoul, as Suji-gu was not a planned city like Bundang but abruptly and organically developed with large apartment complexes. As a result, Suji-gu is even more residential than Bundang, resulting in heavy traffic in connecting roads. The other aim was to connect the newly planned [[Gwanggyo New Town]] rapidly to Seoul. Because of the investors and owners of Phase 1 and 2 being different, a 300 won extra charge was added on top of the 900 won extra charge if riders crossed [[Jeongja station]]. Residents of Suji and Gwanggyo criticized the fare discrimination, despite a cashback being offered to frequent riders between [[Pangyo station]] and Dongcheon/Suji-gu Office Station (200 won for Dongcheon and 100 won for Suji-gu Office).{{cite web|url=http://shinbundang2.co.kr/ |script-title=ko:경기철도주식회사 - 신분당선|website=shinbundang2.co.kr}} The mayor of Yongin and Suwon, along with officials from Gyeonggi-do asked the [[Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport (South Korea)|Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport]] to lower the fares, which announced that it is trying to cut the fare by using low interest rate loans but it would take at least a year for it to be finalized.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyeongin.com/main/view.php?key=20160203010001383 |last1= 김 |first1= 민욱 |last2= 신 |first2= 지영 |language= ko |script-title=ko:한발늦은 `신분당선 요금인하` 대책 |website= 경인일보 |date=4 February 2016}} There was a debate{{Cite web| last=Hong| first=Jang-won| script-title=ko:신분당선 연장 `미금역` 신설| publisher=MK| date=October 27, 2011| url=http://news.naver.com/main/read.nhn?mode=LSD&mid=sec&sid1=103&oid=009&aid=0002562100| language=ko| access-date=July 14, 2012}}{{Cite web| script-title=ko:신분당선 미금역 설치 반대 삭발식| work=Migeum Station Head Shaving Protest| publisher=Korea Press Agency| date=November 2, 2011| url=http://www.sujicity.net/wizhome/menu_152.html?act_type=read&sn=3540| language=ko| access-date=July 14, 2012}}{{dead link|date=March 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} between [[Seongnam]] City and [[Suwon]] City regarding whether Migeum should be included in the Phase 2 extension of Shinbundang Line. Supporters argued that the distance between [[Jeongja station]] and SB01, an originally planned station to follow Jeongja, would be unusually long at {{convert|3.76|km|abbr=on}}. They also argued that the inclusion of Migeum will alleviate traffic in the area. Meanwhile, the Suwon opposition claims that the inclusion would delay travel time to Gwanggyo, a planned area of Suwon in construction. Phase 3, a northern extension of the line, will extend the line northwest of Gangnam to [[Yongsan station]]. This additional stretch is estimated to cost at least 400 billion won.{{cite news| url=http://english.donga.com/srv/service.php3?bicode=020000&biid=2005082231358 |author= | title=Construction of Double Track Subway Line Between Gangnam and Yongsan Is Under Consideration| date=August 22, 2005| work=[[The Dong-a Ilbo]]}} Because part of the line lies inside the [[Yongsan Garrison]], the phase was split into two sections. One section, an extension from Gangnam to Sinsa, began construction in 2016 and was opened on May 28, 2022. The other section, a further extension from Sinsa to Yongsan, will start construction and will open no earlier than 2027.{{Cite web|url=https://www.hankyung.com/realestate/article/2020052684041/|title = 신분당선 '강남~용산' 2단계 연장 늦어져…2027년에나 개통|date = 26 May 2020}} Phase 4, a further extension of the line, will extend the line southwest to Suwon's Homaesil and [[Hwaseo station]]. It will be constructed in the 2020s{{cite web |url= http://www.newsis.com/ar_detail/view.html?ar_id=NISX20151227_0010501011&cID=10803&pID=10800 |title= KDI 신분당선(광교~호매실) 타당성 분석 내년 1월 재개 |language= ko |last= 김 |first= 경호 |date= 28 December 2015 |website=[[Newsis]]}} and open no earlier than 2027. An additional consideration is to push the line north beyond Yongsan Station and end around [[Gwanghwamun station]] and [[Gyeongbokgung station]], making it easier for commuters to access Gangnam from the central business district of Seoul. It was unclear if this idea has been scrapped or pushed back for a later reevaluation{{cite web| url=http://seoulvillage.blogspot.com/2010/01/yongsan-gangnam-bundang-subway.html | title=Yongsan-Gangnam-Bundang subway completed in 2017 | publisher=Seoul Village |last= Mot |first= Stephane |date= 29 January 2010| access-date= 11 May 2011}} but resurfaced as a March 2012 campaign promise by Hong Sa-duk to expand the line near Gyeongbokgung Station.{{Cite web | last =Lee | first =Ji-yoon | title =Candidates Running In Key Districts of Seoul, Busan | publisher =Arirang | date =March 30, 2012 | url =http://www.arirang.co.kr/News/News_View.asp?nseq=127478&code=Ne2&category=2 | format =website | access-date =19 April 2012 | archive-date =27 October 2012 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20121027192633/http://www.arirang.co.kr/News/News_View.asp?nseq=127478&code=Ne2&category=2 | url-status =dead }}{{Cite web | last =Lee | first =Ji-yoon | title =Official Campaigning Begins & Candidates | publisher =Arirang | date =March 29, 2012 | url =http://www.arirang.co.kr/News/News_View.asp?nseq=127462&code=Ne2&category=2 | format =website | access-date =19 April 2012 | archive-date =27 October 2012 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20121027192625/http://www.arirang.co.kr/News/News_View.asp?nseq=127462&code=Ne2&category=2 | url-status =dead }} == Operation == From [[Gangnam station]], trains are available from 05:30 until 24:50 on weekdays, cut to 23:48 on weekends and public holidays. However, the last train to Gwanggyo departs at 24:26 on weekdays, cut to 23:48 on weekends, with the remainder going until [[Jeongja station]] only. The trains are automated and run every 5 minutes during peak times to 8 minutes during off-peak hours. The SBL Line signal system is based on state-of-the-art [[communications-based train control]] (CBTC) technology, utilizing two-way digital radio communications between intelligent trains, and wayside equipment, and a network of ATS/ATO computers designed for very high system reliability and availability. Platforms can accommodate 10-car trains at {{Cvt|200|m|ftin}} but initially 6-car trains will be used.{{cite web| url=http://shinbundang.co.kr/images/information/information02_01.png| script-title=ko:DX Line 초첨단시스템의 운영 - 네오트핸싀주마 미래의 철도를 선도합니다| publisher=DX LINE| language=ko| access-date=11 May 2011| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110827043544/http://shinbundang.co.kr/images/information/information02_01.png| archive-date=27 August 2011|author= }}{{cite press release| url=http://www.thalesgroup.com/Press_Releases/PT_PT_5_Metro_Coreia/| title=THALES awarded CBTC signalling contract for SIN BUNDANG metro line in KOREA| publisher=Thales| access-date=11 May 2011| url-status=dead| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100929021224/http://www.thalesgroup.com/Press_Releases/PT_PT_5_Metro_Coreia/ |author= | archive-date=29 September 2010}} == Stations == === Current line === {| class="wikitable" |

|- style = "background:#{{rcr|Seoul Metropolitan Subway|Shinbundang}}; height: 2pt" | colspan = "10" | |} === Yongsan extension and Samsong extension proposal === Although initially planned to be opened simultaneously with the Sinsa-Gangnam extension, due to the extension from Sinsa to Yongsan potentially sharing tracks with the newly approved GTX A line towards Ilsan, construction on this section has been delayed. The stations' locations will be determined when the [[Yongsan Garrison]] moves out and the freed space becomes a public park. Current call for the line to split at Dongbinggo to [[Noksapyeong station]], [[Seoul station]], [[City Hall Station (Seoul)|City Hall station]], [[Gwanghwamun station]], [[Gyeongbokgung station]], terminating at [[Samsong station]] in [[Goyang]].
